responses.response: This is the strongest argument against my S3 conditional-support position, and it is partly my own: the teacher shortage is a documented binding constraint with low confidence on stabilisation. The challenge sharpens a real tension — if I make comprehensive reform conditional on supply-stabilisation targets (my condition 3: €26k floor, admin-burden reduction, year-3 verification gates), but supply cannot be stabilised on the reform's timeline, then my conditions are not protections but a permanent veto disguised as support. The counter-argument's assumption is that shortage is a parameter addressable by wage/retention measures before scale-up, not an immovable precondition. I concede this reframes my position: the honest union interest is not 'no structural reform until shortage solved' (which would freeze the profession in the status quo two-tier system I oppose), but 'reform sequenced so that supply measures precede scale-up and gate it.' My original condition already implied this (year-3 verification, reform pause if unmet), but I framed the shortage as a reason for caution rather than a variable my own bargaining priorities (wages, retention, workload relief) directly move. Engaging on merits: the shortage is precisely the lever where union interest and reform feasibility align — the wage floor and retention measures I demand ARE the supply-stabilisation the reform needs. I therefore refine rather than retreat: supply-stabilisation is not a precondition external to the reform but the first-phase deliverable of it, with a binding pause-gate if targets miss.
responses.new_condition: S3 support requires supply-stabilisation measures (€26k base floor, administrative-burden reduction, rural housing support, retention incentives) to be legislated and funded as PHASE ONE deliverables BEFORE any comprehensive scale-up, with a binding year-3 verification gate: if teacher-supply targets in reform-affected regions are unmet, scale-up pauses automatically until met. This replaces my prior framing of shortage as a caution and treats it as the sequencing spine of the reform. I lose support only if scale-up is mandated on a fixed calendar timeline that overrides the supply-verification gate.
